claim: A 2022 meta-analysis by Bacci et al. found urban green spaces reduce ambient temperature by 1-5°C with cooling extending 100-500 meters into surrounding neighborhoods, and estimated 2-5 percent heat-mortality reduction per 10 percent green-space increase.

source.title: Urban green space cooling effects: a meta-analysis

method: Reviewed meta-analysis methodology, study inclusion criteria, cooling-effect pooled estimates, and mortality-attribution approach. Focused on geographic distribution of included studies.
limitations: Nearly all included studies are from high-income cities in temperate climates — LMIC tropical and subtropical cities are severely underrepresented.
The 1-5°C cooling range depends on green-space size, vegetation type, irrigation status, and urban morphology — applying it to specific LMIC neighborhoods requires local calibration.
The 2-5 percent mortality-reduction estimate is modeled from cooling-effect data and temperature-mortality concentration-response functions, not directly observed mortality changes.
The meta-analysis does not distinguish between irrigated and unirrigated green space — in water-scarce LMIC cities, unirrigated vegetation provides minimal cooling during heat waves.

claim: Landsat thermal-infrared data at 30-100m resolution provides land-surface temperature for intra-urban heat-island mapping since 1984, enabling neighborhood-scale identification of hotspots and green-space cooling effects.
method: Reviewed Landsat thermal-band documentation for spatial resolution, temporal coverage, atmospheric correction, and validation studies in urban environments.
limitations: Landsat measures land-surface temperature (LST), not air temperature — the relationship between LST and the air temperature that drives heat-stress physiology varies by climate zone, humidity, and urban geometry.
Landsat has a 16-day revisit cycle with cloud-cover gaps — capturing peak heat-wave conditions requires temporal compositing.
The 30-100m resolution may not capture micro-scale cooling effects within individual green spaces or between closely spaced buildings.
LST validation against ground air-temperature stations is sparse in LMIC cities — the LST-to-air-temperature transfer function has not been calibrated for most tropical urban contexts.

claim: Informal settlements in LMIC cities typically have 5-10 times less green space per capita than formal residential areas and surface temperatures 3-8°C higher due to impervious construction materials, building density, and lack of vegetation.

source.title: Green space access and urban heat inequity in LMIC cities

method: Reviewed intra-urban green-space distribution studies, satellite-derived temperature comparisons between formal and informal areas, and vulnerability assessment methodology.
limitations: The 5-10x green-space deficit and 3-8°C temperature difference are ranges across multiple LMIC cities — specific cities may have larger or smaller disparities.
Green-space measurement in informal settlements is complicated by mixed land use, informal gardens, and vegetation that satellite imagery may not detect.
Temperature comparisons between formal and informal areas may confound building-material effects with vegetation effects — disentangling the contributions requires local calibration.
The studies are cross-sectional — they do not establish whether green-space addition in informal settlements would produce the cooling effect predicted by the meta-analysis.

claim: The cooling effect of urban green space depends critically on irrigation — in water-scarce cities, unirrigated vegetation provides minimal cooling during heat waves when cooling is most needed, creating a fundamental constraint for green-space cooling in many LMIC cities.

source.title: Irrigation dependence of urban green space cooling under heat waves

method: Reviewed irrigation-cooling relationship studies, water-scarcity assessments for LMIC cities, and evapotranspiration modeling under heat-wave conditions.
limitations: The irrigation-cooling relationship varies by climate zone, soil type, and vegetation species — local calibration is needed for specific cities.
Water-scarcity assessments are city-level aggregates that may not capture neighborhood-level water availability for green-space irrigation.
The finding that unirrigated vegetation provides minimal cooling during heat waves may not apply to all vegetation types — deep-rooted native species may maintain transpiration under water stress.
Irrigation requirements for green-space cooling may conflict with drinking-water and agricultural water demand in water-scarce LMIC cities.
